text: Characterization of the protein adsorption on nanocarriers and their biological distribution in vivo is crucial for predicting the fate of the nanocarriers in the organism. In the present study, a complete complementary characterization of the adsorption of proteins to hydroxyethyl starch (HES) nanocapsules in solution with both “hard” and “soft” protein corona concepts is shown by exploiting both extractive and non-extractive analytical methodologies. While, by analyzing nanocapsules with the SDS-PAGE and by the protein quantification assays, it was not possible to detect high abundance proteins such as Human Serum Albumin (HSA), in situ analysis with the Isothermal Titration Calorimetry (ITC) and with the Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) have allowed to assess the presence of a soft corona characterized by high stoichiometry and low affinity interaction where the high abundance protein, HSA, has been evidenced a major role as soft corona component, while other proteins, characterized by low affinity but high stoichiometry interaction were not identified. Instead the high affinity protein, Apolipoprotein A-I (ApoA-I) was detected in a stable form as hard corona component. So, by considering the “bioavailability” of nanosystems affected by their interactions with biological media (such as aqueous media that can contain or not a centrifugation step after their addition), these data confirm that a complementary strategy by using or not an extractive centrifugation step is necessary and the soft corona might constitute the major biological part around nanoparticles not easily evidenced in presence of centrifugation step and extraction step typical of the conventional analysis with “biochemical purification”.
explanation: The text uses specialized domain terms and is coherent, but contains grammar oddities and awkward constructions that can hint at AI generation; human authors could produce this too, so it's inconclusive.
